reasoning: The finding is confirmed. The full attack path is: (1) wp_schedule_event registers 'fbrfg_check_for_updates' as a daily WP-Cron action (admin/class-favicon-by-realfavicongenerator-admin.php line 89), with the handler bound at line 43 to check_for_updates() in the admin class, which calls the base class method (common.php line 376). (2) check_for_updates() at line 441 enters the automatic-update branch when $automatic is true and a non-interactive API request is stored. (3) It POSTs to https://realfavicongenerator.net/api/favicon via run_non_interactive_api_request() (common.php lines 484-501) and wraps the raw JSON body in Favicon_By_RealFaviconGenerator_Api_Response with no integrity check. (4) The API response JSON is parsed to extract a package_url (RFG_PACKAGE_URL) that points to a ZIP on realfavicongenerator.net. (5) downloadAndUnpack() (api-response.php line 151) downloads that ZIP via wp_remote_get() and passes it directly to unzip_file() (WordPress's ZipArchive wrapper) with no hash or signature verification. (6) store_pictures() (admin.php line 359) then glob-renames every file from the extracted directory into wp-content/uploads/fbrfg/, overwriting any existing files. No hash_equals, openssl_verify, or sodium_crypto_sign_verify call exists anywhere in the relevant code paths. Severity is downgraded from high to medium because exploitation requires a MitM or compromise of realfavicongenerator.net itself (HTTPS is used), and the destination is wp-content/uploads/fbrfg/ not an executable path by default; however, if the server is misconfigured to execute PHP in uploads, or if the attacker plants a .php file in the ZIP (which is not blocked), arbitrary code execution becomes possible. The reachability claim is correct: no authentication is needed because WP-Cron fires automatically on HTTP requests with no capability check on the cron callback itself.
